body:
> I did not miss that point (see quote at the beginning of your comment 🙂).

@carpben However, the quote consists of two conditions:
- is not in FormData format (there is no such condition ¯\_(ツ)_/¯)
- content-type is multipart/form-data

But there is nothing about plain object type. It doesn't try to detect non FormData type for transformation. Only plain objects, nothing more. Other payloads type just pass through as it was before.

> I thought we need to have a test if a user passes FormData current change doesn't affect it in any way.

FormData is not a plain object type. 
Any object, except: `{}`, `new Object()`, `Object.create(null)` is not a plain object.
That is why that PR could not affect anything, only the case if the user passed a plain object with `multipart/form-data` header to send as JSON string, but this case is erroneous by its nature. It couldn't be used before. So it's should be safe.

Some few standard web things that should be supported:
- [ ] Post/Get Blob/Files

body: IMHO, if Axios moves to a fetch API with Response and Request objects, the existence of this project will become pretty pointless. Users consider one promise per request and cross-platform to be the most important advantages of Axios, but if we do this, we will literally get another fetch library with interceptors. Such projects already exist. Axios should find its own way to simplify the user experience in order to make their day-to-day work easier.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
there are several positive thing by using fetch instead of over XMLHttpRequest.
- Sticking with XMLHttpRequest won't make it work in service worker. Service worker only have the fetch
- Browsers can only stream content using the fetch api. something you can't do with xhr.
- using fetch means axios can work in Deno as well.
- using fetch also means that you do not have to deal with supporting Blob, Files, FormData, URLSearchParams yourself and having to transform or encode FormData yourself...
- NodeJS have now already shipped fetch api into nodejs core.

So it's simply just more easier to just use one adapter instead of having node:http, node:https, xhr and also a fetch adapter to make it work in service worker or cloudflare. it still much possible to have the same existing axios architecture on top of fetch.
Plus they work grate with the cache storage
author: jimmywarting

body:
This is only easy in an ideal world where you can use the latest Node build or browser, but in that case, you could use fetch directly or use some kind of fetch wrapper with interceptors from npm.
Supporting the new adapter and replacing the public Axios API are two different things. Axios is a cross-platform wrapper/facade for adapters with some helpers. Adding a fetch adapter is not a big deal, the challenge is to keep Axios cross-platform with the same API and give it flexibility and extensibility because there are actually more than 2 platforms already and there is no guarantee that in the future we won't want to support another new platform (https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4349).
Also, using fetch without fallback to XMLHttpRequest&http means we are dropping support for rare mobile browsers, legacy browsers, Node older than v18, and proprietary platforms. So in practice, we will have to implement backward compatibility and support at least 3 adapters.

> Is this correct?

Yes, this is correct for Axios v1.0.0-alpha.1

```js
  axios.post("https://httpbin.org/post", data, {
    params: {
      foo: "bar",
      arr: [1, 2, 3]
    },
    paramsSerializer: {
      indexes: null
    }
  });
```
Resolved query string:
```
post?foo=bar&arr=1&arr=2&arr=3
```
[Playground](https://codesandbox.io/s/axios-paramsserializer-indexes-ews7rn?file=/src/index.js)

Summary:
```
indexes: null -> post?foo=bar&arr=1&arr=2&arr=3
indexes: false (default) -> post?foo=bar&arr[]=1&arr[]=2&arr[]=3
indexes: true -> post?foo=bar&arr[0]=1&arr[1]=2&arr[2]=3
```

This should work in Axios v1.0.0. Previously, Axios did not support progress capturing in the server environment, only for browser platforms.
As far as I remember, progress capture for FormData in the node.js environment is not yet supported. There are architectural limitations in the current implementation of the Axios core that prevent this from being added for now. However, capturing the upload/download progress of regular streams should work if the content-size header is specified.
P.S For streams always set maxRedirects to 0 to avoid memory leaks.

One more thing I encountered: `paramsSerializer` structure has changed. Although the new README has a new structure, the official docs site is still referring to the old structure. 
So if you have code like this in your requests:

```diff
axios.get(url, {
- paramsSerializer: (params) =>
-           doSmth(params),
+ paramsSerializer: { serialize: (params) => doSmth }
```

Also make sure you are calling the same HTTPS host that set the cookie.

body:
This method fixes the native `then` method of CancelToken to avoid a memory leak since the native Promise has no way to cancel the subscription added by the `then` chain. This is very important when you are using a persistent token for multiple requests.
This method is not used internally by Axios. It exists for backward compatibility with the CancelToken specification, where CancelToken extends Promise, so it must have a public `then` method. Axios uses proprietary `subscribe` and `unsubscribe` methods to manage subscriptions on CancelToken. They are not part of the spec, but this is a more resource-efficient way of doing that.

body:
`withCredentials` does not create a Windows/NTLM/Kerberos `Authorization` header. It only tells the browser to include credentials that the browser already manages, such as cookies or HTTP auth credentials, when CORS allows it.

For Windows Authentication:
- In the browser, Axios cannot manually send the current Windows credentials. The browser controls Integrated Windows Authentication. The API must be in a trusted/intranet zone, CORS must allow credentials, and the browser/server must negotiate `Negotiate`/`NTLM`.

- In Node.js, Axios does not implement NTLM/Kerberos itself. You would need a dedicated agent/library that supports the auth scheme, or call through a backend/proxy that handles Windows auth.
So this is not something `withCredentials` alone can enable.

body:
Axios is an isomorphic library. This error indicates that your build tool is not configured properly to build the client-related code, instead, it tries to build the Node.js version of Axios and then use it on the client, polyfilling the node.js dependencies. Axios for browsers does not have any dependencies.
So to fix this, you need to figure out how to configure your bundler to resolve the client-side code of the dependencies.

By doing this you remove this alert but a new appears:

I use yarn berry and I got a similar warning. I fixed it by adding the following config to `.yarnrc.yml`. Once you're done, remember to run `yarn` to apply the change. 

```
packageExtensions:
  axios@*:
    dependencies:
      debug: "*"
  debug@*:
    dependencies:
      supports-color: "*"
```

body:
## Solution: Use TypeScript `import type` keyword

This issue occurs because Axios 1.x changed how it exports TypeScript types. The solution depends on your setup:

### **Recommended Solution (for TypeScript projects):**

Add the `type` keyword to your import statement:

```typescript
import type { AxiosResponse, AxiosRequestConfig } from 'axios';
import axios from 'axios';
```

The `type` keyword tells TypeScript that you're importing only types (which are removed at compile time), not runtime values. This prevents ESLint's `import/named` rule from complaining since no actual exports are being imported.

### **Why this happens:**

In Axios 1.x, `AxiosResponse` is only exported as a type, not as a named export. The error occurs when ESLint tries to validate that the named import exists as a runtime value, which it doesn't.

### **Alternative Solutions:**

1. **Disable ESLint rule** (not recommended):
   ```javascript
   // .eslintrc.js
   rules: {
     'import/named': 0,
   }
   ```

2. **Use eslint-import-resolver-ts** for better TypeScript support:
   - Install: `npm install --save-dev eslint-import-resolver-ts`
   - Configure `.eslintrc.js`:
   ```javascript
   settings: {
     'import/resolver': {
       typescript: true,
     }
   }
   ```

### **Summary:**
The cleanest approach is using `import type` – it's TypeScript best practice and requires no config changes.

body:
This usually points to a module interop/build issue rather than Axios itself.

Axios v1 is published as ESM with conditional exports, and Babel transpiling only your source files can sometimes produce code that expects a `.default` export shape that the consuming package/runtime does not actually provide.

Things to check:

```js
// If your built output is CommonJS, try:
const axios = require('axios');
```

or enable Babel/TypeScript interop consistently, for example `esModuleInterop` / `allowSyntheticDefaultImports` on the TypeScript side, or make sure Babel is not rewriting ESM imports in a way your consuming package cannot execute.

Also make sure `axios` is declared as a dependency or peer dependency of the package that uses it, not only of the consuming app.

A practical workaround is to avoid publishing half-transpiled module output: either keep ESM output as ESM, or build a proper CJS bundle that handles interop consistently.

`axios-es6-class` is a third-party package, so its peer dependency range is controlled by that package, not by Axios.

If `axios-es6-class@4.2.2` declares a peer dependency on `axios@0.20.0`, your safe options are:

- Upgrade `axios-es6-class` if it has a newer version that supports the Axios version you want.
- Ask/submit a PR to `axios-es6-class` to widen its peer dependency range.
- Remove/replace `axios-es6-class` if it is no longer maintained.
- Use your package manager's peer-dependency override/resolution only after testing, because that bypasses the package author's compatibility declaration.

Also note that `axios@0.20.0` is very old. If you are upgrading Axios, consider moving to the latest `1.x` version and checking the migration guide rather than only `0.21.0`.

body:
Axios does not guarantee request ordering. Each `axios.post()` is an independent HTTP request, and once multiple requests are in flight, network scheduling, connection reuse, retries, server processing, Firebase function concurrency, and cold starts can all make them arrive or complete out of order.

Using Node's `https` module directly is unlikely to solve that by itself; it has the same underlying networking behavior.

If ordering matters for synchronization, you need to enforce it at the application level, for example:

- Include a monotonically increasing version/sequence number with each document change.
- Make `server2` apply only newer versions and ignore/retry stale ones.
- Queue changes per document/key so only one request for that document is in flight at a time.
- Use a durable queue/pub-sub system instead of relying on arrival order of concurrent HTTP requests.

Also, Axios `0.27.2` and Node 16 are both old, so upgrading is a good idea, but it should not be treated as the ordering fix.

body:
`responseType: 'arraybuffer'` tells Axios not to treat the response body as text/JSON. It keeps the response as raw bytes.

For a gzipped report, there are two separate things involved:

1. `Content-Encoding: gzip`
   This is HTTP-level compression. In Node.js, Axios' HTTP adapter normally decompresses this automatically unless `decompress: false` is set.

2. `responseType: 'arraybuffer'`
   This controls how the already-received body is represented to your code. With `arraybuffer`, you get bytes instead of decoded text.

If you are recreating this outside Axios, make sure you are doing both pieces correctly:

- Accept/read the response as binary data, not a string.
- If the server sends `Content-Encoding: gzip`, decompress the bytes with gzip before interpreting the report.

In Node.js that usually means using `zlib.gunzip`/`gunzipSync` or piping through `zlib.createGunzip()`, depending on how you are reading the response.

body:
Axios cannot directly enable or configure HTTP keep-alive in React Native the way it can in Node.js.

The Node.js examples usually use `httpAgent` / `httpsAgent`:

```js
import http from 'node:http';
import https from 'node:https';

axios.create({
  httpAgent: new http.Agent({ keepAlive: true }),
  httpsAgent: new https.Agent({ keepAlive: true })
});
```

That only works with Axios’ Node http adapter. In React Native, Axios uses the XHR-style adapter and delegates networking to React Native’s native networking stack. So httpAgent, httpsAgent, and Node socket pooling options do not apply.

In practice, connection reuse in React Native is controlled by the native layer:
- iOS: usually NSURLSession
- Android: usually OkHttp

Those native clients may already reuse connections, but Axios does not expose a JS-level option to force or tune that behaviour.
A few things to check:
- Make sure every request uses the exact same scheme, host, and port.
- Check whether the server or proxy sends Connection: close.
- Confirm HTTP/2 is actually negotiated from the React Native app, not only from browsers.
- Compare with React Native fetch() from the same app. If fetch() has the same latency, the issue is below Axios.
- Use server logs, a proxy, or packet capture to verify whether a new TLS handshake is happening on every request.
- Do not try to set Connection: keep-alive manually. It is not useful for HTTP/2 and may be ignored or blocked by the platform.

If you need explicit control over connection pooling in React Native, that usually requires native configuration rather than Axios configuration. For example, on Android, you may need to customise the app’s OkHttpClient; on iOS, you may need to customise the networking layer around NSURLSession.

So the short answer is: Axios itself cannot make React Native reuse connections. React Native’s native networking stack is responsible for that.

Por lo que se ve, esto no parece ser un problema de Axios directamente. Parece más bien un problema de renderizado en React, de CSS, o un error de JavaScript que está deteniendo la aplicación antes de mostrar los componentes. Lo primero que revisaría es la consola del navegador: 1. Abrí DevTools. 2. Entrá en la pestaña **Console**. 3. Revisá si hay errores en rojo. 4. También mirá la pestaña **Network** si tus componentes dependen de datos cargados con Axios. Cosas comunes que pueden causar esto: - `App` no está siendo renderizado correctamente en `main.jsx` o `index.jsx`. - El componente devuelve `null`, un fragmento vacío o una condición que nunca se cumple. - Hay un error en algún componente y React deja de renderizar. - El CSS está ocultando el contenido, por ejemplo `height`, `overflow`, `display`, `position`, `z-index`, etc. - El `root` del HTML no coincide con el que usás en React. - Una request con Axios falla y el componente no maneja el estado de `loading` / `error`. Ejemplo básico que debería existir en `main.jsx` o `index.jsx`: ```jsx import React from 'react'; import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client'; import App from './App.jsx'; ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')).render( ); ``` Y en `index.html` debería existir: ```html
``` También podés probar temporalmente que React esté renderizando algo básico: ```jsx function App() { return (

React está funcionando

); } export default App; ``` Si eso se ve, entonces el problema está dentro de alguno de tus componentes o estilos. Si eso tampoco se ve, el problema probablemente está en la configuración inicial de React, el `root`, o algún error global. Sí, sería útil que compartas el repo o al menos estos archivos: - `package.json` - `index.html` - `src/main.jsx` o `src/index.jsx` - `src/App.jsx` - los componentes principales - el CSS global - el error completo de la consola del navegador, si aparece

body:
The difference is where in the merge pipeline each one lives.

When axios makes a request, it merges headers in this order (each step can override the previous):

1. `defaults.headers.common` - sent on every method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.)
2. `defaults.headers[method]` - sent only for that HTTP method (e.g. `defaults.headers.post`)
3. Per-request headers you pass directly

When you do `axios.create({headers: {Accept: '...'}})`, that `Accept` goes into a slot that behaves like step 1 but is separate from `defaults.headers.common`. This is why the two values don't interfere with each other - they're different inputs to the merge.

In practice: use `defaults.headers.common` for things like `Authorization` that should go on every request regardless of method. Use the method-specific slots for things that only make sense on certain methods (like `Content-Type` on POST/PUT). The create-time headers are useful for defaults scoped to a specific client instance.

```js
const instance = axios.create({ baseURL: 'https://api.example.com' })

// these two are equivalent for "send on all methods":
instance.defaults.headers.common['Authorization'] = `Bearer ${token}`
// vs
instance.defaults.headers['Authorization'] = `Bearer ${token}`

// method-specific:
instance.defaults.headers.post['Content-Type'] = 'application/json'
```

The `common` key is just the conventional name axios uses internally - it's not magic, it's just processed first in the merge chain.

body:
This does not look like an Axios-specific problem. The key clue is:

```txt
self-signed certificate
```

That means the request is failing during the HTTPS/TLS handshake before your API route is reached. So your .NET API may appear to be “not being called” because Node rejects the local development certificate before an HTTP request completes.

If this is only local development, the simplest fix is to call the HTTP endpoint instead of HTTPS:

```ts
const apiURL = 'http://localhost:5000/api/your-endpoint';
```

Check the exact ports printed by your .NET app when it starts:

```txt
Now listening on: http://localhost:5000
Now listening on: https://localhost:5001
```

If you need to use HTTPS locally, trust the .NET development certificate:

```sh
dotnet dev-certs https --trust
```

Then restart both the .NET API and the Remix dev server.

For Axios, you can temporarily disable certificate validation in local development only:

```ts
import axios from 'axios';
import https from 'node:https';

const api = axios.create({
  baseURL: 'https://localhost:5001',
  httpsAgent: new https.Agent({
    rejectUnauthorized: false
  })
});
```

Do not use `rejectUnauthorized: false` in production. It disables TLS certificate verification.

Also, since this code runs server-side in Remix, CORS is probably not the issue. CORS applies to browser requests. A Remix loader/action running in Node is making a server-to-server request.

A few other things to check:

- If Remix is running in Docker, `localhost` means the Remix container, not your host machine. Use `host.docker.internal` or the Docker service name.
- Make sure the API URL uses the correct protocol and port: `http://localhost:5000` vs `https://localhost:5001`.
- Try calling the API from the same environment as Remix using `curl`.
- Try `127.0.0.1` instead of `localhost` if there is an IPv4/IPv6 binding issue.
- For a `GET` request, you usually do not need `Content-Type: application/json` because there is no request body.

For debugging Axios errors, avoid `JSON.stringify(err)`. Axios errors contain useful fields that may not serialise well. Log this instead:

```ts
if (axios.isAxiosError(err)) {
  console.log({
    message: err.message,
    code: err.code,
    status: err.response?.status,
    data: err.response?.data,
    url: err.config?.url
  });
}
```

So the likely fix is: use the HTTP localhost URL during development, or make Node trust the local .NET HTTPS certificate.

body:
The issue is almost certainly that `name` is a **special built-in property** on JavaScript functions and class constructors.

If your data object is a **class instance**, the `name` property on the class constructor is non-enumerable — meaning when Axios serializes the object, it may resolve `name` from the prototype chain instead of your instance field, resulting in `null`.

**Fix: spread to a plain object before sending:**

```ts
put<T = any, R = AxiosResponse<T>>(
  url: string,
  data?: T,
  config?: AxiosRequestConfig
): Promise<R> {
  return this.axiosInstance.put<T, R>(url, { ...data } as T, config);
}
```

Or use a JSON round-trip to strip the prototype entirely:

```ts
return this.axiosInstance.put(url, JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(data)), config);
```

**Why this happens:**
- `name` is a reserved, non-enumerable property on `Function.prototype`
- Class instances inherit from their constructor, so `instance.name` can silently resolve to the constructor name instead of your data field
- Spreading (`{ ...data }`) creates a plain object with only own enumerable properties, bypassing the prototype chain

You can verify by running: `Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(YourClass, "name")` — if it shows `writable: false`, this is the cause.

Hi
the Browser Security Restriction that is Browsers intentionally hide specific CORS error details from JavaScript code, causing Axios to return a generic "Network Error" (Status 0) for both CORS blocks and actual network failures (like DNS issues).

How to Differentiate them is that  You can distinguish a CORS/Network error from a Bad Request (4xx) by checking if error.response exists; if error.response is undefined, the request was blocked (CORS) or failed to reach the server.

Code Logic that you can apply is that Use if (!error.response) to identify these "blocked" requests, whereas actual HTTP errors (like 400 or 404) will always contain the response object with the status code.

This is a race condition where your initial request is firing before the interceptor configuration code has actually executed .
In the JS modules , the imports are hoisted , but the logic inside them runs in order  so if your request triggers immediately then it might beat the interceptor registration .
I think to fix this issue , you should create a dedicated api.js file that sets up the instance and interceptors and then exports that configured instance to use in your app , ensuring that the setup is always happens first .

body:
This error is probably not caused by `axios.get()` itself.

```txt
Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'call')
```

inside `__webpack_require__` usually means Webpack tried to execute a module id that is missing from the loaded bundle/chunk. That is commonly caused by a bundling/deployment/cache issue, not an Axios runtime API issue.

A few things to check:

## 1. Remove runtime aliases first

Do not alias Axios to the Node/CJS build in the browser unless you have a specific reason.

Use the normal import:

```ts
import axios from 'axios';
```

Avoid browser app aliases like:

```js
axios: 'axios/dist/node/axios.cjs'
```

If you absolutely need an alias for a browser build, use the browser bundle:

```js
resolve: {
  alias: {
    axios: require.resolve('axios/dist/browser/axios.cjs')
  }
}
```

But I would first try without any alias.

## 2. Upgrade Webpack

`webpack@5.52.1` is quite old. Axios 1.x uses modern package `exports` / conditional exports. Webpack 5 supports this, but older Webpack 5 versions had a number of resolver/runtime issues.

Try upgrading Webpack and related tooling:

```sh
npm install webpack@latest webpack-cli@latest --save-dev
```

Then delete the build output and rebuild from scratch.

## 3. Check for stale deployed chunks

Because this happens after build/deploy, make sure the browser is not loading an old chunk with a new entry bundle, or the reverse.

This is a very common cause of `__webpack_require__(...).call` / missing module errors.

Check that:

- Your deploy deletes old build files before uploading new ones.
- JS filenames include content hashes.
- `index.html` is not cached too aggressively.
- CDN/browser cache is invalidated after deploy.
- All chunks from the same build are deployed together.

For example, `index.html` should usually not be cached long-term:

```http
Cache-Control: no-cache
```

But hashed JS files can be cached long-term:

```http
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
```

## 4. Check for duplicate/mismatched Axios versions

Run:

```sh
npm ls axios
```

Make sure you do not have both Axios `0.x` and `1.x` bundled through different dependencies.

If using Module Federation/micro-frontends, also make sure host and remotes are not mixing different Axios builds. In that case, configure Axios as a shared singleton or redeploy all remotes/hosts together.

## 5. Do not use Jest config as browser config

This part:

> For testcases fix we have axios.cjs in module mapper

is fine for Jest if needed, but it should not affect your browser bundle unless the same alias/module mapper is also being used by Webpack.

Keep Jest-only config separate from Webpack runtime config.

## 6. Simplify the Axios call

This is not the cause of the Webpack error, but the code can be simplified:

```ts
const res = await axios.get(url);

const versions = res.data?.Versions;
const latest = versions?.[versions.length - 1];

if (latest?.URL) {
  window.open(appUrl + latest.URL, '_blank');
}
```

## Most likely fix

Given the error location is `__webpack_require__`, I would focus on:

1. Remove Axios aliases.
2. Upgrade Webpack from `5.52.1`.
3. Clean `node_modules`, lockfile, and build output.
4. Rebuild and redeploy all chunks atomically.
5. Clear CDN/browser cache for `index.html`.

If the issue still happens, the useful next detail would be the full stack trace plus the generated module id Webpack is trying to load when `__webpack_require__` fails.

hi ,
you are actually passing the headers as the request body . In axios.post , the second argument is reserved for the data payload , while the third argument is for config object .
To fix this issue you should pass an empty object { } as the second argument , and move your headers into the third argument.
The correct syntax should be like :
axios.post(url, {}, { header: { ... } } )

Appreciate the detailed report I will look into this

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026, 7:12 AM Engy Park ***@***.***> wrote:

> The second parameter of request interceptor only runs when a previous
> interceptor returns Promise.reject(), not when a runtime error occurs
> inside the first parameter.
> Why
>
> Axios interceptors work as a Promise chain internally.
> The second parameter is a .catch() handler for the *previous
> interceptor's rejected promise*, not a try/catch for the current one.
>
> So in your code, username.length throws a TypeError at runtime, which
> bypasses the chain entirely and is never caught by the second parameter.
> How to actually trigger the second parameter
>
> // interceptor 1 - intentionally rejectsinstance.interceptors.request.use(
>   (config) => Promise.reject(new Error("rejected from interceptor 1")));
> // interceptor 2 - second parameter receives the rejection from interceptor 1instance.interceptors.request.use(
>   (config) => config,
>   (error) => {
>     console.log("now this runs!", error); // runs
>     return Promise.reject(error);
>   });
>
> If you want to catch runtime errors inside a single interceptor, use
> try/catch explicitly.
>
> instance.interceptors.request.use(
>   (config) => {
>     try {
>       let username = null;
>       console.log(username.length);
>     } catch (error) {
>       return Promise.reject(error);
>     }
>     return config;
>   },
>   (error) => {
>     console.log("now this runs!", error); // runs
>     return Promise.reject(error);
>   });

body:
Axios doesn’t provide built‑in retry functionality, but the recommended approach is to use a retry helper such as axios-retry, which integrates with Axios through interceptors.
First install it:

```
npm install axios-retry
```

Then configure it:

```
import axios from 'axios'
import axiosRetry from 'axios-retry'

const api = axios.create()

axiosRetry(api, {
  retries: 3,
  retryDelay: axiosRetry.exponentialDelay,
  retryCondition: (error) => {
    return axiosRetry.isNetworkError(error) ||
           axiosRetry.isRetryableError(error)
  }
})
```
Now requests will automatically retry up to 3 times for network errors or retryable server responses.

Example usage:

```
const res = await api.get('/users')
console.log(res.data)
```

Using a dedicated retry helper is usually preferable to implementing manual retry logic because it keeps the request layer cleaner and leverages Axios interceptors properly.
